chunk_summary: Guidance on managing discordant MUAC and WHZ/WLZ results in undernutrition screening, emphasizing measurement verification, oedema assessment, and a stepwise approach to classification and care decisions.
learning_objectives: Understand the reasons for discordance between MUAC and WHZ/WLZ in malnutrition screening.
Learn the stepwise approach to verify measurements and classify severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Identify criteria for inpatient versus outpatient care in SAM management.
Recognize the importance of follow-up plans and predefined failure triggers in outpatient care.
key_facts: MUAC and WHZ/WLZ capture overlapping but distinct high-risk groups, leading to discordant results.
Step 1: Verify measurement quality, including MUAC tape tension and length/height positioning.
Step 2: Presence of bilateral pitting oedema classifies as SAM regardless of other indices.
Step 3: Apply 'worst-indicator wins'—manage as SAM if either MUAC or WHZ/WLZ meets the threshold.
Step 4: Complicated SAM (danger signs, medical instability, failed appetite test) requires inpatient care.
Outpatient care for stable SAM cases requires weekly follow-ups and predefined failure triggers.
reference_quote: Discordant MUAC and WHZ/WLZ results are expected because the indicators capture overlapping but not identical high-risk groups. Mortality comparisons between 'MUAC-only' and 'WHZ-only' severe wasting vary by dataset and design; therefore neither indicator should replace the other, and discordance should be managed conservatively. (WHO guideline)
document_key_topics: Discordance between MUAC and WHZ/WLZ in malnutrition screening
Stepwise approach to SAM classification and management
Criteria for inpatient versus outpatient care in SAM
Follow-up and monitoring in outpatient SAM care
